text: This robotic video highlights a total pelvic accentuation with a caloanal anastomosis and an intra-assisted plaster urological reconstruction.
We report the case of a fifty-nine-year-old male diagnosed with locally advanced mid-rectal cancer involving the urogenital apparatus, staged as a MRT-IV-BN-II-EMVI-positive.

He was treated with induction chemotherapy with full sarcosophageal fironauts followed by chemoradiotherapy prior to undertaking a robotic total pelvic accentuation with coloanal anastomosis and endorhizoplasty.
The aims of this video are to highlight the key salient steps involved in this procedure.
The robotic pelvic accentuation begins with a transanal approach, where a full-thickness rectotomy is performed approximately one centimeter proximal to the dentate line.
The surgeon enters the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane from the transanal approach, initiating dissection in the pelvic region.

This approach is favoured when undertaking complex robotic accentuation as it facilitates distal transection in a controlled manner should there be any intra-abdominal or pelvic complications during the robotic phase.

The surgeon enters the TME plane posteriorly and continues dissection down to the pelvic floor, aiming to connect with the previous transanal dissection. The dissection progresses circumferentially along the right and left lateral TME planes.

The surgeon identifies and carefully dissects the left ureter, maintaining a cuff of tissue around it to preserve vascular integrity. The dissection extends from the pelvic brim to the vesico-ureteric junction, ensuring no ischemic compromise for the planned ileo-ureteric anastomosis.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the right ureter, ensuring a cuff of tissue is maintained around it to preserve vascular integrity, similar to the procedure performed on the left side.

The right ureter is visible as it crosses the bifurcation of the common iliac artery. The surgeon carefully maintains a cuff of tissue around the ureter while dissecting it down to the vesico-ureteric junction.
The surgeon mobilizes the urinary bladder anteriorly, opening the pre-vesicle space of Retzius. The pubic bone becomes visible in the anterior view.

The previously dissected left ureter is visible. The surgeon waits to transect it until the bladder is fully mobilized, aiming to cut the ureter as distally as possible near the vesico-ureteric junction to preserve maximum length for anastomosis.

The surgeon continues bladder mobilization laterally and inferiorly, reaching the endopelvic fascia. An energy device is used to divide the inferior and superior vesicle vascular pedicles, with visible separation of the vascular structures.
The left vas deferens is visible and is divided using a robotic energy device.
The endopelvic fascia is visible, demonstrating its reflection over the levator ani muscle.
The surgeon transects the right ureter near the right ureterovesical junction, similar to the left side, and proceeds to mobilize the right lateral bladder.
The endopelvic fascia reappears in the surgical field and is incised. The prostate is mobilized, and the urethra is identified within the operative area.
The surgeon performs a urethrotomy, dividing the urinary catheter. Subsequently, the dorsal venous complex is sutured to achieve haemostasis, with visible control of bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the dissection, confirmed visually by transanal digital examination.
The surgeon exteriorizes the previously mobilized colon through the anus, with approximately five centimeters of the colon visible outside the body to facilitate a delayed coloanal anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a robotic urological enterocystoplasty, isolating a 30-centimeter ileal segment located approximately 20 centimeters from the ileocecal valve.

The surgeon performs an enterotomy and constructs the posterior wall of an ileourethral anastomosis. The proximal and distal segments of the isolated ileal segment are transected using a stapler, followed by detubularization of the ileal segments.

The surgeon opens the small bowel segment along its anti-mesenteric border, extending down to the previously formed ileo-urethral anastomosis. The second segment of the small bowel is divided, reaching the iliourethral anastomosis.
The surgeon restores gastrointestinal continuity by performing a side-to-side stapled anastomosis using a robotic stapler, with two firings visible during the procedure.
The surgeon detubularizes the second half of the ileal segment along its anti-mesenteric border, visibly opening up the bowel segment.

The surgeon sutures the medial aspects of the detubularized ileal segments together, forming the posterior wall of the neobladder. The corners of these segments are folded down to the level of the ileo-urethral anastomosis, creating a spherical shape for the neobladder.
The surgeon uses a self-locking barbed suture to construct the neobladder, visibly folding the detubularized ileal segments to form a spherical shape.
The surgeon forms the anterior aspect of the neobladder by suturing the lateral aspects of the isolated ileal segments. The anterior wall of the ileo-urethral anastomosis is also being constructed.
The spherical shape of the neobladder becomes visible as the superior aspect is closed. The surgeon prepares to perform the ileo-ureteric anastomosis once the pouch is fully formed.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the corner of the neobladder and performs an end-to-end ileo-ureteric anastomosis. A double J stent is inserted to protect the anastomosis site.
The surgeon locates the previously dissected left ureter and places a second double J stent robotically into the left ureter, extending into the pouch of the neobladder.
The completed urological anastomosis is visible, showing the results of the pelvic accentuation with coloanal anastomosis and urological enterocystoplasty reconstruction of the bladder.

Postoperatively, the patient was enrolled into our pelvic accentuation enhanced recovery programme and had an event for recovery. His histology was a T-for-B-N-naught-R-naught resection. At three months' follow-up, he had recovered well from his operation, had a last score of ten, and had no urinary symptoms.
